ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for mint-infused young coconut drink; closest match is coconut water food product. Mint infusion and pulp component are not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX covers coconut water; mint infusion and young coconut pulp are not separately coded. Used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for mint-infused young coconut beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g (one cup or bottle), providing about 75 kcal, 17 g carbohydrate, and 100 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Coconut water (SR Legacy #12117) and Spearmint, fresh (SR Legacy #02065); values weighted to reflect a young coconut water and mint beverage blend
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 21.43 kcal, protein 0.43 g, fat 0.14 g, carbohydrate 4.86 g) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for coconut water (#12117: energy 19 kcal, protein 0.72 g, fat 0.20 g, carbohydrate 3.71 g) and are plausible for a young coconut water and pulp blend with mint. Values adopted from input with USDA SR Legacy as corroborating approved source. Potassium estimated at 195 mg/100g based on USDA SR Legacy coconut water data. Singapore HPB Foodo database consulted as secondary regional source; no exact entry for this beverage found. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water_g set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and moisture breakdown for this blended beverage are not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy or HPB database.

gi_source.title: Coconut water, GI ~54 — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for coconut water and peer-reviewed literature (Lau et al., 2020, Asia Pacific Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for mint-infused young coconut drink. GI of 54 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for coconut water (GI ~54, low category). Mint contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (54 × 17.01) / 100 ≈ 9.2. Carbohydrate per serving = 4.86 g/100g × 350 g = 17.01 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (28.6 mg/100g) and naturally high potassium (~195 mg/100g); no added sugar or salt
hypertension.guidance: Minty young coconut drink is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its low sodium and high potassium content align with DASH dietary principles, and one standard serving (350 g) contributes only ~100 mg sodium — well within the 2,000 mg daily limit — while providing potassium that supports vasodilation; avoid adding sugar syrups or salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~54), naturally occurring sugars only (4.3 g/100g), no added sugar, moderate carbohydrate (4.86 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Minty young coconut drink has a low glycemic index (~54) and contains only naturally occurring sugars with no added sweeteners, making it acceptable for individuals with type 2 diabetes in moderate portions; one serving (350 g) provides approximately 17 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~9.2, which is within a manageable range, but total daily carbohydrate intake should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.09 g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, low sodium, and potassium-rich electrolyte profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Minty young coconut drink is cardioprotective in profile: it contains negligible saturated fat (0.09 g/100g), no trans fat, no cholesterol, and a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio that supports healthy blood pressure; it is suitable as a hydrating beverage for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, provided no sugar or cream is added.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mint polyphenols (rosmarinic acid) offer mild anti-inflammatory benefit; however, no strong anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, >3 g fibre, cruciferous vegetables) are present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Minty young coconut drink is not contraindicated for rheumatoid arthritis and mint's rosmarinic acid provides mild anti-inflammatory polyphenols, but the beverage alone does not supply sufficient anti-inflammatory factors (omega-3 fatty acids, high whole-food fibre, or cruciferous phytonutrients) to be considered therapeutically beneficial; it may be consumed as part of a broader anti-inflammatory dietary pattern without added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Minty Young Coconut Drink is a refreshing chilled beverage originating from Singapore, made from fresh young coconut water and tender young coconut pulp infused with fresh mint leaves. The drink is minimally processed — the coconut is cracked or opened, the water and soft jelly-like pulp are combined, and fresh mint is steeped or blended in before chilling. The result is a light, subtly sweet, and cooling drink with a clean herbal finish from the mint. Young coconut water is naturally isotonic, rich in potassium (~195 mg/100g), and low in calories (21 kcal/100g), making it a popular hydration choice in tropical Southeast Asian climates. The addition of mint contributes rosmarinic acid and menthol, providing mild anti-inflammatory and digestive benefits. Nutritionally, the beverage is very low in fat (0.14 g/100g), low in sodium (28.6 mg/100g), and contains naturally occurring sugars (4.3 g/100g) with no added sweeteners. Its glycemic index is approximately 54 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The drink is halal, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly served in hawker centres, cafes, and grab-and-go outlets across Singapore and Malaysia, often in a cup or bottle of approximately 350 g. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or syrups.
common_user_queries: Is young coconut drink good for diabetics?
Is coconut water with mint good for blood pressure?
Can I drink coconut water if I have hypertension?
Young coconut drink carbohydrates per serving

confidence_reason: Input per_100g values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for coconut water and are plausible for this blended beverage; however, no exact approved-source entry exists for mint-infused young coconut drink as a composite product. Values are adopted from the input with USDA SR Legacy as corroborating evidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for coconut water. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this blended young coconut and mint beverage is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (#12117) or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific blended preparation is not reported in the approved sources consulted. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy coconut water data; plant-based beverage with no hydrogenated fats. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; plant-based beverage contains no cholesterol. 5. potassium_mg estimated at 195 mg/100g based on USDA SR Legacy coconut water (#12117 reports ~203 mg/100g); value is plausible and consistent with the input record's reference to potassium. 6. Input ethnic_tags included 'Laksa' and 'Nasi Lemak' which are unrelated dishes and have been removed. Primary cuisine corrected from 'Malaysian' to 'Singaporean' reflecting the country_of_origin field value of Singapore; the beverage is common across both Singapore and Malaysia. 7. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for coconut water; no direct GI study for mint-infused young coconut drink was identified in the approved GI source. 8. Glycemic load per serving = (54 × 17.01) / 100 ≈ 9.2, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 4.86 × 3.5 = 17.01 g. 9. All aliases in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array (q8 self-check passed).
